era_context: The early 11th century saw the fragmentation of the Abbasid Caliphate, the rise of the Fatimid Caliphate in North Africa and the Levant, the consolidation of feudal kingdoms in Western Europe following the collapse of Carolingian authority, the flourishing of the Song Dynasty in China, and the expansion of Hindu kingdoms in South India. This was a period of religious ferment, with the Christian Church in the West undergoing reform movements, Islam expanding in Southeast Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, and Buddhism declining in its Indian heartland.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; oral traditions and archaeology underrepresented)
Southeast Asia (sparse contemporary documentation; reliance on later chronicles and epigraphy)
Northern Europe beyond Scandinavia and the Frankish sphere (limited literacy)

events.title: Henry II crowned Holy Roman Emperor
events.description: Henry II, Duke of Bavaria, was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope John XVIII on February 14, 1004, following the death of Otto III in 1002. Henry's election and coronation consolidated German royal authority and marked the beginning of the Salian dynasty's rise. His reign would be defined by conflicts with the papacy, struggles to maintain imperial authority in Italy, and military campaigns against Poland and Hungary. The coronation reaffirmed the symbolic union of temporal and spiritual authority in Western Christendom.

sources.name: Thietmar of Merseburg, Chronicon (early 11th century chronicle)

events.certainty_note: The coronation date is well-attested in multiple near-contemporary chronicles. Henry's election in 1002 and coronation in 1004 are securely dated.
cross_references: Follows from 1002-001 (Otto III's death and Henry's election)

events.title: Fatimid-Byzantine naval conflict in the Mediterranean
events.description: Escalating tensions between the Fatimid Caliphate and the Byzantine Empire over control of Mediterranean trade routes and coastal territories resulted in naval skirmishes and raids. The Fatimids, under Caliph Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah, sought to expand their naval power and protect their commercial interests. These conflicts were part of a broader pattern of Mediterranean competition that would intensify throughout the 11th century, affecting Christian-Muslim relations and trade networks.
events.certainty_note: The broader pattern of Fatimid-Byzantine conflict in this period is well-established, but specific events in 1004 are not sharply dated in surviving sources. The year is assigned by historiographic convention.

events.title: Song Dynasty consolidation under Emperor Zhenzong
events.description: Emperor Zhenzong of Song (r. 997–1022) continued the administrative and military consolidation of the Song Dynasty, which had reunified much of China after the fragmentation of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period. In 1004, the Song court was engaged in ongoing military campaigns against the Khitan Liao Dynasty in the north and managing internal administrative reforms. The Song period was marked by technological innovation, including advances in printing, naval technology, and military engineering, as well as the flourishing of Neo-Confucian philosophy.
key_figures: Empress Dowager Liu (regent and political advisor)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang, 11th century
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty), compiled in the 13th century

events.certainty_note: The Song Dynasty's political and administrative activities in 1004 are well-documented in Chinese historical records. Zhenzong's reign dates and major policies are securely established.

events.title: Chola naval expansion in the Indian Ocean
events.description: The Chola Empire under Rajendra I (r. 985–1014) was in the midst of its greatest territorial expansion, with naval expeditions extending Chola power across the Indian Ocean to Sri Lanka, the Maldives, and Southeast Asia. In 1004, the Cholas were consolidating their control over maritime trade routes and establishing themselves as a dominant naval power. This period saw the construction of major temples, including the Brihadeshvara Temple at Thanjavur (completed c. 1010), and the development of sophisticated administrative systems. Chola expansion had profound effects on the spread of Hindu culture and Tamil language throughout the Indian Ocean region.

sources.name: Chola inscriptions and temple records (epigraphic evidence)
Later Tamil chronicles and literary works

events.certainty_note: The broad chronology of Rajendra I's reign and naval expansion is well-established through inscriptions and later chronicles. Specific events in 1004 are less precisely dated but fit within the documented expansion period.

events.title: Persecution of Christians in Egypt under Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah
events.description: Caliph Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (r. 996–1021) initiated a period of religious persecution against Christians and other non-Muslim minorities in Fatimid Egypt. In 1004, restrictions on Christian worship, church construction, and Christian officials in the administration were intensified. These policies, which would culminate in the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem in 1009, reflected both theological intolerance and political efforts to consolidate Islamic identity within the Fatimid realm. The persecution caused significant social disruption and prompted some Christian emigration.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-Hunafa (15th-century compilation of earlier sources)

events.certainty_note: Al-Hakim's persecution of Christians is well-documented, but the precise chronology of individual acts is often uncertain. The year 1004 is assigned based on the general timeline of escalating persecution in the early 1000s.
cross_references: Leads to 1009-001 (destruction of Church of the Holy Sepulchre)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Henry II's coronation as a turning point in imperial history has been somewhat overstated in older historiography. Modern scholarship emphasizes that his authority was contested and limited, particularly in Italy, and that the 'Salian dynasty' was not a coherent political force until later in the 11th century. Additionally, the Fatimid-Byzantine conflict in 1004 is often presented as a major geopolitical confrontation, but recent scholarship suggests that trade and diplomatic relations were more significant than military conflict in this period. The persecution of Christians under Al-Hakim is well-attested, but some sources conflate events from different years, and the extent of systematic persecution versus sporadic violence remains debated.
historiographic_note: The year 1004 is moderately well-documented in European and East Asian sources, but documentation is uneven. Western European sources are dominated by monastic chronicles with a clerical perspective and often focus on ecclesiastical and dynastic events. Chinese sources are rich and detailed but concentrate on court politics and administrative matters. Islamic sources for the Fatimid period are often later compilations, and contemporary documentation is sparse. South Asian sources are primarily epigraphic and architectural, limiting narrative detail. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are virtually absent from contemporary documentation. The surviving record is heavily biased toward elite political and religious events; economic, social, and technological developments are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Henry II's coronation as emperor followed from his election as king in 1002 after Otto III's death
Escalating persecution of Christians under Al-Hakim culminated in the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in 1009
Song Dynasty consolidation in China and Chola expansion in South Asia were concurrent developments in different regions
